learning_objectives: Define interactive storytelling and distinguish it from linear narrative media
Identify the core elements that make interactive fiction unique: agency, consequence, emergent meaning
Survey the history of interactive narrative from text adventures to modern AAA titles
Analyze the tension between authored narrative and player freedom
readings: Game Narrative Toolbox by Heussner et al., Ch. 1-2
Interactive Storytelling for Video Games by Lebowitz and Klug, Ch. 1
Hamlet on the Holodeck by Janet Murray, Ch. 1 (Introduction)

lecture_notes.introduction: Welcome to Interactive Storytelling, a course that explores the unique challenges and possibilities of telling stories in a medium where the audience is also a participant. Interactive narrative is not simply a story with choices bolted on -- it is a fundamentally different form of storytelling that must account for player agency, branching consequence, emergent meaning, and the tension between authorial vision and player freedom. Unlike a novelist who controls every word the reader encounters, or a filmmaker who controls every frame the viewer sees, an interactive storyteller creates a possibility space within which each player constructs their own unique narrative experience. This requires a different set of skills from traditional writing: systems thinking to manage branching complexity, empathy to anticipate player expectations, technical literacy to implement narrative in code, and the humility to accept that the player's story may diverge from the one you intended. Over the next fifteen weeks, we will study narrative theory, build branching stories in professional tools, design dialogue and quest systems, and create a polished interactive narrative experience for your portfolio.
key_concepts: Ludology vs narratology: the academic debate between games-as-systems and games-as-stories, and why both perspectives are necessary
Ergodic literature and cybertext theory: Espen Aarseth's framework for texts requiring non-trivial effort to traverse, with games as the primary example
Player agency as narrative force: the degree to which player choices meaningfully alter outcomes and how perceived agency differs from actual branching
Diegetic vs non-diegetic storytelling: narrative elements that exist within the game world (environmental storytelling, NPC dialogue) vs outside it (HUD text, narrator voice)
The narrative spectrum: from fully linear (no player influence on story) through branching to fully emergent (procedurally generated narrative from systems)
Authored vs emergent narrative: the difference between a writer's crafted story and the stories that emerge from player interaction with game systems
Interactive fiction history: Zork (1977), Choose Your Own Adventure (1979), Myst (1993), BioWare dialogue wheels, Telltale Games, modern narrative indie games
The paradox of choice: too few choices feel railroaded, too many choices cause decision paralysis and content bloat
Narrative coherence under player control: maintaining thematic and tonal consistency when the player drives the story in unexpected directions
Course project overview: building toward a polished interactive narrative experience using the toolchain of your choice
lecture_notes.demonstration: Play-through comparison: experiencing the same story beat in The Walking Dead (branching player choice), Gone Home (environmental discovery), and Final Fantasy VII (linear with optional content), analyzing how each approach creates narrative engagement through fundamentally different mechanisms of player involvement
setup_requirements: Twine 2 installed (twinery.org) for rapid narrative prototyping
Ink scripting language and Inky editor (inklestudios.com)
GitHub account for project submissions and version control
Familiarity with at least one narrative-driven game from the provided list

review.value: The degree to which a player's choices meaningfully alter narrative outcomes and their subjective experience of the story, encompassing both actual and perceived influence
Text requiring non-trivial effort from the reader to traverse, as defined by Espen Aarseth -- games are a primary example where the reader must make decisions to access content
Narrative delivered through elements that exist within the game world (NPC dialogue, found documents, environmental details) rather than through external UI or narration
Stories that arise organically from player interaction with game systems rather than from authored scripts, such as unplanned events in simulation games

class_schedule.topic: Story Structure: Three-Act and Hero's Journey
learning_objectives: Apply three-act structure and the Hero's Journey to interactive narrative design
Adapt linear story structure frameworks for branching and non-linear delivery
Identify the structural elements that remain constant across branches versus those that vary
Design story beats that function regardless of the path the player took to reach them
readings: The Writer's Journey by Christopher Vogler, Ch. 1-4
Story by Robert McKee, Ch. 8-10 (Structure)
Into the Woods: A Five-Act Journey Into Story by John Yorke, Ch. 2

lecture_notes.introduction: Every story, interactive or not, needs structure -- a beginning that hooks, a middle that escalates, and an ending that resolves. The three-act structure and Joseph Campbell's Hero's Journey provide time-tested frameworks for organizing narrative beats into a satisfying arc. But interactive stories face a unique challenge: the player controls the pacing. They might rush through Act 1, linger in Act 2, or reach the climax from an unexpected direction. Adapting traditional story structure for interactive media means identifying which structural elements are fixed (the inciting incident must happen, the climax must occur) and which can flex based on player choices (the order of Act 2 challenges, the specific path to the climax, the nature of the resolution). Today we study how to build a structural skeleton that holds together regardless of the player's path through the story, using techniques like mandatory story gates, flexible middle acts, and convergent climax nodes. The goal is not to force the player onto a predetermined path but to create a structure robust enough that every path leads to a satisfying experience.
key_concepts: Three-act structure: Setup (world/character/conflict establishment), Confrontation (escalating obstacles), Resolution (climax and denouement)
Hero's Journey in games: Call to Adventure (tutorial/hook), Crossing the Threshold (entering the game world), Trials (gameplay loops), Ordeal (midpoint crisis), Return (climax and resolution)
Story gates: mandatory narrative beats that all paths must pass through, ensuring structural integrity regardless of player choices
Flexible middle act: allowing players to encounter Act 2 content in variable order while maintaining escalating tension through gating mechanisms
Convergent climax: funneling diverse player paths to a climactic encounter that acknowledges accumulated choices without requiring unique content per path
Pacing in interactive media: the player controls traversal speed, so pacing must be embedded in level design and encounter spacing rather than forced timing
Dramatic irony in games: the player can know things the character does not, and vice versa, creating tension unique to interactive media
Theme as structural glue: when branching paths diverge in plot, a consistent thematic question holds the experience together
Midpoint reversal: a revelation or shift at the story's midpoint that reframes everything before it, motivating engagement through Act 2
Denouement and epilogue: resolving character arcs and world state after the climax, with epilogue content reflecting accumulated player choices
lecture_notes.demonstration: Structural analysis of The Witcher 3's Bloody Baron questline: mapping its three-act structure, identifying the mandatory story gates versus optional side content, showing how the player can reach the climactic choice from different information states, and analyzing how the multiple endings each provide a satisfying resolution to the same thematic question about family and violence
setup_requirements: Story structure template worksheets (provided: three-act and Hero's Journey diagrams)
Twine 2 for hands-on structural prototyping
Whiteboard or graph paper for structural diagramming

review.value: Setup (establish world/character/conflict), Confrontation (escalating obstacles and complications), Resolution (climax and denouement) -- the foundational narrative framework adapted for interactive delivery
Mandatory narrative beats that all player paths must pass through, ensuring structural integrity and plot coherence regardless of the player's specific route through branching content
A narrative design technique where diverse player paths funnel to a climactic encounter that acknowledges accumulated choices through variation rather than requiring entirely unique content
Using a consistent thematic question across all branches to maintain narrative coherence even when plot details diverge significantly based on player choices

learning_objectives: Map the major branching narrative architectures and evaluate their trade-offs
Implement state tracking variables that accumulate player choice history
Design foldback structures that manage combinatorial complexity while preserving agency
Calculate content budgets for different branching architectures
readings: Game Narrative Toolbox Ch. 3-4
Salen and Zimmerman, Rules of Play Ch. 26 (Games as Narrative Play)
Ink Documentation: Variables and Logic

lecture_notes.introduction: Every interactive story has an underlying architecture that determines how branches split, merge, and resolve. The architecture you choose is the most consequential design decision in interactive narrative because it directly determines your content scope, development cost, and the type of player experience you can deliver. A pure binary branching tree doubles content at every decision point -- with just 10 choices, you need 1,024 unique endpoints, an impossible content burden for any team. Professional interactive narratives use sophisticated architectural patterns to create the feeling of meaningful choice without exponential content growth. State tracking is the key technology: instead of creating unique branches for every combination of choices, you track the player's decisions as variables and use those variables to modify content at key moments. The player who helped the villagers in Chapter 2 sees a different line of dialogue in Chapter 5, but the plot structure remains the same. Today we study the major branching architectures, implement state tracking in Ink, and learn to calculate whether a narrative design is feasible within a given content budget.
key_concepts: Binary branching trees: exponential content growth (2^n unique paths for n choices), illustrating why naive branching is infeasible at scale
Foldback structures: branches diverge then reconverge at key plot nodes, giving players agency between gates while limiting total unique content
Hub-and-spoke: a central location (hub) with optional side content (spokes) that can be completed in any order before advancing the main plot
Parallel path narratives: two or three distinct plot threads that run simultaneously, with the player choosing focus order but experiencing all
Quality-based narrative (QBN): the Fallen London model where available content is filtered by accumulated stat values rather than explicit branching
State variables: integers, booleans, and strings tracking player choices, relationship values, quest completion, and world state across the full narrative
Conditional content: lines, paragraphs, or entire scenes that appear or change based on state variable values, personalizing the experience without unique branches
Content budget calculation: estimating total word count for a branching structure based on branch points, foldback frequency, and conditional variation
The illusion of choice: choices that feel meaningful to the player but converge to the same outcome, reducing content cost while maintaining engagement
Delayed consequences: choices whose effects appear much later in the narrative, creating a sense of a world that remembers and responds to the player
lecture_notes.demonstration: Implementing a state-tracked narrative in Ink: building a three-scene story with two branching points that track choices as variables, using conditional text to personalize dialogue based on prior choices, implementing a foldback point where both paths converge but with state-dependent variation in how the character reacts, and calculating the total word count showing how this approach uses 40% less content than pure binary branching for equivalent perceived choice
setup_requirements: Inky editor (Ink IDE) installed and configured
Ink variable and logic syntax reference sheet (provided)
Graph paper or draw.io for branching architecture diagrams

review.value: A branching architecture where paths diverge for a scene or chapter then reconverge at mandatory plot nodes, managing scope while preserving the experience of meaningful choice
Named data values (integers, booleans, strings) tracking accumulated player choices and world state, used to conditionally modify content rather than creating unique branches
The exponential growth of unique story paths when every choice creates a permanent branch; the primary feasibility constraint of branching narrative design
A narrative architecture where content availability is determined by accumulated stat values rather than explicit branching, enabling high variability with linear content growth

learning_objectives: Compare dialogue system architectures: menu-based, keyword, parser-based, and hybrid
Write dialogue that communicates character personality within branching constraints
Implement a multi-character dialogue scene in Yarn Spinner with conditional branches
Design conversation flow that balances information delivery with player expression
readings: Writing for Games by Hannah Nicklin, Ch. 5-6
Yarn Spinner documentation: Writing with Yarn Spinner tutorial
Disco Elysium postmortem: Dialogue System Architecture (GDC 2020)

lecture_notes.introduction: Dialogue is the most common vehicle for interactive storytelling and the place where player agency is most directly exercised. When a player selects a dialogue option, they are simultaneously expressing their interpretation of the character, gathering information about the world, and making decisions that may affect the narrative. A well-designed dialogue system balances three competing needs: expressiveness for the writer (enough flexibility to create nuanced conversation), clarity for the player (clear consequences of each choice), and tractability for the developer (a system that scales without exponential complexity). Today we examine the major dialogue system architectures, from the Mass Effect conversation wheel to Disco Elysium's skills-as-characters approach, and implement a complete dialogue scene in Yarn Spinner -- the narrative middleware increasingly adopted by indie studios for its Unity and Godot integration. We also study the craft of writing dialogue for games: how to convey character personality in the constrained format of dialogue choices, how to balance exposition with entertainment, and how to write options that are meaningfully different rather than cosmetically varied.
key_concepts: Menu-based dialogue: the player selects from visible options (Mass Effect wheel, Disco Elysium list), the most common and accessible approach
Parser-based dialogue: the player types responses in natural language (classic text adventures, some modern experiments), high freedom but low predictability
Bark systems: short context-sensitive NPC lines triggered by game events without entering a dialogue interface, for ambient world-building
Dialogue state machines: conversation nodes connected by edges gated by conditions (player stats, inventory, prior choices, relationship values)
Yarn Spinner syntax: nodes defined by title, body text, choices with [[option|destination]] syntax, variables with <<set>> and <<if>> commands
Writing meaningful choices: each option should represent a distinct player intent (aggressive, diplomatic, curious, deceptive) not just different phrasings of the same thing
Character voice consistency: maintaining distinct speech patterns across all dialogue branches, even when different writers contribute to the same character
Information architecture in dialogue: controlling what the player learns and when, using dialogue as a pacing mechanism for revelation and mystery
Voice-over production constraints: how recording budgets limit branching depth and require careful line planning, word count estimation, and recording scripts
Dialogue testing: verifying all paths through a conversation are reachable, all variable states are handled, and no dead-end nodes exist
lecture_notes.demonstration: Building a complete dialogue scene in Yarn Spinner: a negotiation between the player and an NPC merchant where the available dialogue options change based on the player's reputation variable, a persuasion check determines whether a discount is offered, the conversation branches based on whether the player has a specific quest item, and the final outcome (deal, no deal, or conflict) is tracked in a state variable for future reference -- all implemented in Yarn Spinner syntax with proper node organization
setup_requirements: Yarn Spinner installed (standalone editor or Unity/Godot integration)
Sample dialogue template from course repository
Character voice reference sheet (provided: 3 character profiles with speech patterns)

review.value: A graph of dialogue nodes connected by edges whose traversal is gated by conditions such as player stats, inventory items, relationship values, or prior conversation choices
Short context-sensitive lines spoken by NPCs in response to game events without entering a full dialogue interface, maintaining gameplay flow while building world atmosphere
Dialogue options that represent genuinely distinct player intents (aggressive, diplomatic, curious) rather than cosmetic variations of the same response
Maintaining distinct speech patterns, vocabulary, and personality traits for each character across all dialogue branches, ensuring the character feels real regardless of the player's path

learning_objectives: Design game worlds that communicate history, culture, and conflict through their construction
Build coherent fictional worlds with internal consistency and discoverable depth
Create world bibles that guide consistent storytelling across a development team
Balance world-building depth with development scope constraints
readings: Wonderbook by Jeff VanderMeer, Ch. 3 (World-building)
Game Narrative Toolbox Ch. 6-7
World Building in Games: The Narrative Design of Dishonored (GDC talk)

lecture_notes.introduction: A game world is not just a setting -- it is a character in itself, with history, values, conflicts, and secrets. World-building for games differs from world-building for novels or film because the player can explore the world at their own pace and in their own direction. This means the world must be consistent at every point of contact: the architecture, the NPC conversations, the item descriptions, the environmental details must all tell the same coherent story about what this world is and how it works. A poorly built world reveals its inconsistencies to curious players; a richly built world rewards exploration with deeper understanding. Today we study the techniques of systematic world-building: creating foundational documents (world bibles) that define history, geography, factions, technology, and culture; designing worlds that communicate their nature through visual and environmental cues rather than exposition dumps; and managing the practical challenge of building a convincing world within development constraints. The key principle is the iceberg theory: you must know ten times more about your world than the player will ever see, because that invisible depth informs every visible detail.
key_concepts: The iceberg principle: world-building depth that the player never directly sees but that gives consistency and richness to every visible detail
World bible structure: history timeline, geography, factions and power structures, technology level, cultural norms, economic systems, creation myths
Environmental storytelling as world-building: architecture, technology, art style, signage, and decay patterns that communicate world rules without text
Faction design: groups with distinct values, aesthetics, and goals whose conflicts drive the narrative forward and give the player meaningful allegiances
Technology and magic systems: establishing clear rules for what is possible in the world and communicating those rules through consistent application
Cultural world-building: language fragments, naming conventions, holidays and rituals, social hierarchies that make fictional cultures feel authentic
Historical layering: visible evidence of past events in the present world (ruins, monuments, battle scars, cultural practices that reference forgotten origins)
Scope management: deciding how deep to build each aspect of the world based on what the player will actually encounter vs what exists only in the bible
Lore delivery mechanisms: found documents, NPC dialogue, codex entries, environmental details, loading screen text -- each with different engagement characteristics
World consistency checking: systematic review processes to catch contradictions in world rules, timeline, and character knowledge across a large narrative
lecture_notes.demonstration: Building a world bible for a fictional game setting: defining the core conflict (two factions with irreconcilable values), establishing the technological and social context, creating a 500-year timeline that explains the current political situation, designing three discoverable lore items (a letter, a monument inscription, and a propaganda poster) that each reveal different aspects of the same historical event from different factional perspectives, and showing how this world knowledge informs consistent environmental storytelling decisions
setup_requirements: World bible template document (provided: structured sections with prompts)
Mood board creation tools (Pinterest, Miro, or physical collage materials)
Reference: Dishonored Heart system as world-building vehicle

review.value: A comprehensive reference document defining a game world's history, geography, factions, technology, culture, and rules, serving as the single source of truth for all narrative content
Building world-building depth far beyond what the player directly encounters, so that visible details are consistently informed by invisible underlying knowledge
Creating groups with distinct values, aesthetics, resources, and goals whose conflicts generate narrative tension and give players meaningful allegiance choices
Making past events visible in the present world through ruins, cultural practices, and artifact remnants, creating a sense of a world with genuine history

learning_objectives: Apply environmental storytelling techniques to convey narrative without dialogue or text
Design spaces that communicate character, event history, and emotional tone through spatial arrangement
Analyze environmental storytelling in published games and identify specific techniques used
Create environmental narrative designs that guide player discovery without explicit direction
readings: Environmental Storytelling in Games by Don Carson (Gamasutra article)
Game Narrative Toolbox Ch. 8
Level Design: Processes and Experiences, Ch. 5 (Spatial Storytelling)

lecture_notes.introduction: Environmental storytelling is the art of telling stories through space, objects, and their arrangement. A blood trail leading to a barricaded door tells a story without a single word. A child's drawing pinned to a refrigerator in an abandoned apartment tells another. A desk with half-eaten food and an overturned chair tells a third. The power of environmental storytelling is that it respects player agency: the narrative is there for players who want to find it, but it does not interrupt players who want to keep moving. It transforms passive observation into active discovery, making the player a detective rather than an audience member. This form of storytelling is particularly powerful in games because it leverages the medium's unique strength: spatial exploration. No other narrative medium lets the audience walk through a space, examine objects from any angle, and piece together a story from physical evidence at their own pace. Today we study the techniques professional designers use to embed narrative in environments, from broad atmospheric storytelling (the mood of a space) to specific narrative communication (this exact event happened here).
key_concepts: Mise-en-scene applied to games: arranging environmental elements with cinematic intentionality to communicate mood, character, and narrative
Found narrative objects: journals, audio logs, photographs, graffiti, personal belongings that reveal character and plot when discovered
Spatial progression: guiding the player through a narrative arc by controlling their path through spaces that escalate in tension or revelation
Object placement as evidence: positioning objects to tell a causal story (knocked over furniture implies struggle, packed bags imply planned departure, rotting food implies sudden departure)
Lighting as narrative: pools of light guide attention to story-critical objects; darkness hides secrets; color temperature communicates safety vs danger
Scale and proportion for emotional impact: cavernous spaces create awe or vulnerability; tight spaces create claustrophobia or intimacy; ruins at human scale emphasize loss
Layered environmental history: showing multiple time periods in a single space (medieval foundation, Victorian renovation, modern decay) to communicate passage of time
Player-created environmental narrative: leaving evidence of the player's own passage (opened doors, defeated enemies, moved objects) for a sense of personal history
Audio as environmental storytelling: distant sounds, echoes, music leaking through walls that communicate the presence or absence of life beyond the visible space
Contrast and juxtaposition: placing conflicting elements together (a child's toy next to a weapon) to create narrative tension through visual dissonance
lecture_notes.demonstration: Walkthrough analysis of environmental storytelling in Gone Home and Dark Souls: cataloguing every narrative object in a single room from Gone Home (15+ items), mapping what each communicates about the characters and story, then comparing with a Dark Souls bonfire area where environmental storytelling uses enemy placement, item location, and architecture to communicate world lore rather than personal narrative; followed by a class exercise designing the environmental storytelling for an abandoned classroom in a post-apocalyptic game
setup_requirements: Screenshot reference sets from Gone Home, Dark Souls, BioShock, and What Remains of Edith Finch
Room layout template for design exercise (provided: isometric grid)
Environmental storytelling checklist (provided: object categories and narrative functions)

review.value: Story elements embedded in the environment as discoverable objects (letters, recordings, photographs, personal items) that the player pieces together into a coherent narrative
Using the arrangement, progression, and character of spaces to guide the player through a narrative arc, with each room or area advancing the story through its environmental design
Positioning environmental objects to communicate causal narratives: overturned furniture implies struggle, packed bags imply departure, personal items imply occupation
Placing conflicting environmental elements together to create narrative tension and invite the player to reconcile the contradiction (a child's drawing near evidence of violence)

learning_objectives: Write characters with consistent voice and personality across branching narrative paths
Design character arcs that function within player-driven story structures
Distinguish between defined protagonist arcs and blank-slate avatar projection
Create companion characters that serve as mirrors and commentators on player choices
readings: The Writer's Journey by Christopher Vogler, Ch. 5-8 (Archetypes)
Interactive Storytelling for Video Games Ch. 8
Character Development and Storytelling for Games by Lee Sheldon, Ch. 4-5

lecture_notes.introduction: Characters in interactive stories face a fundamental tension: the writer wants to craft a specific transformation arc, but the player wants ownership of who the character becomes. The best interactive stories resolve this tension not by eliminating it but by designing characters and systems that channel player choice into narratively satisfying outcomes regardless of direction. A defined protagonist like Geralt of Rivia has an established personality that the player influences but does not determine; a blank-slate avatar like the Courier in Fallout: New Vegas is a vessel for player projection with minimal authored personality. Neither approach is inherently superior -- each serves different design goals. Today we study the craft of writing characters for interactive media: creating voices that remain consistent across branches, designing arcs that adapt to player choices, building companion characters that react to and comment on player decisions, and the technical challenge of maintaining character coherence when the player can encounter content in any order.
key_concepts: Defined protagonist vs blank-slate avatar: the spectrum from fully authored character personality to empty vessel for player projection
Character voice document: defining speech patterns, vocabulary, emotional range, and verbal tics for each character to ensure consistency across writers and branches
Arc-lock points: narrative moments where accumulated player choices crystallize into a committed character trajectory, reducing future branching
Companion characters as moral mirrors: NPCs who reflect, challenge, or reinforce the player's choices, providing external perspective on player decisions
Moral alignment systems: tracking player choices on ethical dimensions (paragon/renegade, honor/chaos) and reflecting accumulated alignment in character behavior
Character consistency under branching: techniques for maintaining personality coherence when the player can encounter scenes in different orders
NPC memory: characters that reference prior interactions, remembered choices, and changed attitudes based on relationship tracking variables
Ensemble dynamics: designing character relationships that create tension, comedy, and drama through conflicting values and goals within a group
Player emotional attachment: techniques for creating characters the player cares about (vulnerability, competence, humor, shared adversity, sacrifice)
The three-dimensional character: every significant character needs a public self (how they present), a private self (who they really are), and a core belief that drives both
lecture_notes.demonstration: Comparative analysis of character arc design in The Witcher 3 (defined protagonist who the player steers) vs Fallout: New Vegas (blank-slate who the player defines), mapping how each handles player agency in character development; then a writing exercise where students write the same scene for both a defined and a blank-slate protagonist, experiencing the different constraints and possibilities firsthand
setup_requirements: Character voice document template (provided: speech pattern categories and examples)
Character arc worksheet (provided: setup, catalyst, trials, climax, resolution)
Twine project from previous sessions for character writing exercise

review.value: A character with pre-established personality, backstory, and voice whose arc the player influences but does not wholly determine (e.g., Geralt of Rivia, Commander Shepard)
A narrative moment where accumulated player choices commit the character to a specific trajectory, reducing future branching while crystallizing a coherent arc
An NPC character designed to reflect, challenge, or validate player choices, providing external perspective and emotional consequence to the player's decisions
A reference guide defining each character's speech patterns, vocabulary, emotional range, and verbal tics to ensure consistency across writers and content branches

learning_objectives: Design choices that feel genuinely meaningful to the player through consequence and visibility
Distinguish between actual agency (branching outcomes) and perceived agency (presentation of choice)
Implement the delayed consequence pattern for long-term narrative impact
Evaluate choice design in published games and identify successful and failed implementations
readings: Game Narrative Toolbox Ch. 5
The Art of Game Design by Jesse Schell, Ch. 24 (Story and Game Structures)
The Craft and Science of Game Design by Philippe O'Connor, Ch. 7 (Player Choice)

lecture_notes.introduction: The central promise of interactive storytelling is that the player's choices matter. Breaking this promise -- through choices that have no visible consequence, options that all lead to the same outcome, or decisions whose effects are invisible -- is the fastest way to disengage a player from your narrative. But creating truly meaningful choices is expensive: every unique outcome requires unique content. The art of interactive narrative design is finding the sweet spot where the player feels maximum agency with manageable content scope. Today we study the anatomy of meaningful choice: what makes a choice feel significant to the player, how to design consequences that are visible and satisfying, how to use the delayed consequence pattern to create the illusion of a deeply responsive world, and the controversial technique of the 'illusion of choice' -- where different options converge to the same outcome but the player's experience of making the choice still has narrative value. We will analyze choice design in published games, identifying the techniques that create genuine agency and the shortcuts that merely simulate it.
key_concepts: Anatomy of a meaningful choice: clear options, distinct outcomes, visible consequences, alignment with player values, and irreversibility
Types of choice: moral dilemmas (no good option), strategic decisions (trade-offs), expressive choices (character definition), informational choices (what to learn)
Actual vs perceived agency: the difference between choices that change the plot and choices that change the player's experience of the same plot
The illusion of choice: options that converge to the same outcome but feel different to the player -- ethically and practically evaluated
Delayed consequences: choices whose effects appear scenes or chapters later, creating a sense of a world that remembers and responds
Immediate feedback: visual, audio, or NPC reaction that confirms a choice was registered, even if the major consequence is delayed
Choice presentation: how the framing, timing, and context of a choice affects its perceived importance (timed choices, unreversible confirmations)
Moral complexity: designing dilemmas where both options have genuine costs, avoiding simplistic good/evil categorization
The ripple effect pattern: a single choice producing cascading consequences across multiple systems (faction reputation, NPC relationships, available quests)
Choice fatigue: the diminishing engagement when too many choices of low consequence are presented, and how to pace choices for maximum impact
lecture_notes.demonstration: Analyzing choice design in three games: (1) Mass Effect 2's suicide mission where every earlier choice about crew loyalty determines who lives and dies -- genuine agency with devastating consequences; (2) Telltale's The Walking Dead where many choices converge but the experience of making them carries emotional weight -- effective illusion of choice; (3) Fallout 4's dialogue wheel where four options frequently lead to the same outcome with no emotional investment -- failed choice design; followed by designing a single meaningful choice node with immediate feedback and delayed consequences in Ink
setup_requirements: Inky editor for choice implementation exercise
Game footage clips from Mass Effect 2, The Walking Dead, and Fallout 4 (provided)
Choice design evaluation rubric (provided: 8 criteria for meaningful choice assessment)

review.value: A decision with clear options, distinct outcomes, visible consequences, value alignment, and irreversibility that makes the player feel their decision genuinely shaped the narrative
A choice whose effects appear scenes or chapters later, creating the sense of a world with memory and the expectation that every decision may have future implications
Options that converge to the same narrative outcome but give the player the emotional experience of making a decision, controversial but widely used when content budget is limited
Diminished engagement from too many low-consequence choices, mitigated by pacing significant choices and reducing trivial ones

learning_objectives: Structure quests that serve both gameplay and narrative goals simultaneously
Design quest chains with meaningful branching and visible consequences
Implement quest dependency graphs for non-linear quest availability
Balance quest pacing with player freedom in open-world narrative structures
readings: Game Narrative Toolbox Ch. 9-10
Quest Design in The Witcher 3 (GDC 2017 talk by Pawel Sasko)
Yarn Spinner documentation: Quest System Integration

lecture_notes.introduction: Quests are the primary unit of narrative delivery in most games beyond pure interactive fiction. A well-designed quest weaves story, gameplay, and player choice into a single coherent experience. The quest structure provides a natural container for narrative content: a hook that grabs attention, objectives that create direction, complications that generate tension, and a resolution that provides closure. But quest design in modern games is more complex than a simple task list. Players expect quests to acknowledge their choices, connect to the broader world narrative, and offer multiple approaches and outcomes. Today we study the craft of quest design: structuring individual quests for maximum narrative and gameplay engagement, chaining quests into storylines with cumulative consequences, building quest dependency graphs that enable non-linear discovery, and managing the practical challenge of quest systems in open-world games where the player can encounter content in any order.
key_concepts: Quest anatomy: hook (what draws the player in), objectives (what they must do), complications (what goes wrong), resolution (how it ends), reward (what they gain)
Quest types: main story quests (advance the central plot), side quests (expand the world), faction quests (deepen allegiance), companion quests (develop character relationships)
Quest chains: sequences of quests where completion of one unlocks the next, with branching based on how prior quests were resolved
Quest dependency graphs: directed acyclic graphs showing prerequisite relationships between quests, enabling non-linear availability
Multiple solution paths: designing quests solvable through combat, stealth, dialogue, or creative approaches, rewarding player expression
Quest consequences: how quest resolution affects the world (NPC state changes, area modifications, faction reputation shifts, future quest availability)
Open-world quest pacing: managing narrative coherence when the player can defer quests indefinitely, encounter them out of intended order, or ignore them entirely
Side quest as world-building: using optional content to deepen the world rather than just provide XP, making side quests feel essential even when optional
Quest journal and tracking: designing UI that communicates current objectives, tracks accumulated progress, and surfaces relevant information without spoilers
Localization impact on quest design: how translation requirements constrain quest text volume, dynamic text insertion, and culturally specific content
lecture_notes.demonstration: Designing a complete three-quest chain: a village quest line where the initial quest (investigate missing livestock) has two solution paths (track the predator or interrogate suspicious villagers), the second quest depends on the first's resolution (hunting expedition or confrontation), and the third quest brings consequences home (village reactions based on accumulated choices) -- implemented as a dependency graph in Yarn Spinner with state variables tracking all decision points
setup_requirements: Yarn Spinner or Ink editor for quest implementation
Quest design template document (provided: anatomy, dependency, and branching worksheets)
Graph paper or draw.io for quest dependency diagrams

review.value: A directed acyclic graph showing prerequisite relationships between quests, enabling non-linear quest availability based on player progression, choices, and world state
Designing quests that can be completed through different approaches (combat, stealth, dialogue, exploration), rewarding diverse play styles and creating replay value
World state changes resulting from quest completion: NPC attitudes shift, areas are modified, new quests become available, and faction relationships adjust based on player decisions
Designing optional content that deepens world understanding and emotional investment rather than just providing mechanical rewards, making side quests feel meaningful

learning_objectives: Design cutscenes that advance narrative without breaking player engagement
Balance cutscene-driven storytelling with interactive narrative delivery
Apply cinematic techniques (camera, editing, composition) to game cutscene design
Evaluate when cutscenes are the right tool versus when interactive delivery is superior
readings: Directing the Story by Francis Glebas, Ch. 4-6
Game Writing Handbook by Rafael Chandler, Ch. 7 (Cinematics)
Half-Life 2 Commentary: Interactive Storytelling Without Cutscenes

lecture_notes.introduction: Cutscenes are the most cinematic tool in the game narrative toolkit and the most controversial. At their best, they deliver emotional moments with the production value of film -- characters emote with facial animation, cameras frame dramatic compositions, and the writer controls pacing precisely. At their worst, they are interruptive, disempowering interludes that strip the player of the agency they came for. The key question for every cutscene is: could this information be delivered interactively instead? If the answer is yes, the cutscene is usually the wrong choice. Cutscenes earn their existence when they deliver something interactive gameplay cannot: close-up character performance, precisely timed dramatic reveals, physical action the player cannot perform, or emotional beats that require controlled pacing. Today we study cutscene design as a narrative tool: when to use them, how to design them for maximum engagement, how to integrate them with interactive narrative so they feel like part of the experience rather than interruptions, and how games like Half-Life 2 and God of War achieve cinematic storytelling with minimal or zero traditional cutscenes.
key_concepts: Cutscene types: pre-rendered (highest quality, no player influence), real-time (uses game engine, can reflect player state), interactive (QTEs, walking sections, choices during scenes)
The cutscene contract: the player trades control for production value and narrative payoff; the cutscene must justify the trade
Cinematic camera techniques: shot composition (rule of thirds, leading lines), shot types (establishing, medium, close-up, POV), camera movement (dolly, pan, crane)
Editing principles for game cinematics: shot-reverse-shot for dialogue, cutting on action, maintaining spatial orientation, pacing for emotional beats
Integration with gameplay: cutscenes that reflect player choices (character appearance, companion presence, dialogue variations), seamless camera transitions
The Half-Life approach: no cutscenes, all story delivered in real-time while the player retains movement and camera control -- advantages and limitations
QTEs and interactive cutscenes: maintaining player engagement during scripted sequences through timed inputs, choices, or partial movement control
Cutscene writing: script format for game cinematics including camera direction, character blocking, and technical notes for the implementation team
Skippable and pausable: respecting player time by making cutscenes skippable on replay and pausable for interruptions
Performance capture integration: how motion capture and facial capture data is edited and refined for in-engine cutscene delivery
lecture_notes.demonstration: Analyzing cutscene integration in three games: (1) God of War (2018) -- one-shot camera with seamless gameplay-to-cutscene transitions; (2) Mass Effect 2 -- conversation cutscenes that reflect player choices in shot composition and dialogue; (3) Metal Gear Solid V -- minimal cutscenes with most narrative delivered through audio tapes and environmental storytelling; followed by storyboarding a 60-second game cutscene with camera direction, blocking, and dialogue, then evaluating whether the same content could be delivered interactively
setup_requirements: Storyboard template sheets (provided: 6-panel and 12-panel layouts)
Game footage clips showing different cutscene approaches (provided)
Screenplay format reference for game cinematics (provided)

review.value: The implicit agreement where the player surrenders control in exchange for production value and narrative payoff -- the cutscene must justify the trade or risk player disengagement
The technique of moving from gameplay camera to cinematic camera without a visible cut, maintaining immersion and the feeling that the player is still in the world
A scripted narrative sequence that maintains partial player control through movement, camera, timed inputs, or dialogue choices during the cinematic moment
Delivering all narrative in real-time within the game world while the player retains movement and camera control, never removing agency for story purposes

learning_objectives: Understand how procedural systems generate narrative through emergent player experiences
Design narrative systems that produce meaningful stories from combinatorial mechanics
Evaluate the trade-offs between authored narrative and procedural narrative generation
Implement a simple procedural narrative event system with modular story components
readings: Procedural Storytelling in Game Design by Tanya Short and Tarn Adams, Ch. 1-3
Emergent Narrative in Dwarf Fortress (GDC talk by Tarn Adams)
AI Dungeon and GPT-based narrative generation: possibilities and limitations

lecture_notes.introduction: Procedural narrative is the frontier of interactive storytelling: stories that no author wrote, emerging from the interaction of systems, rules, and player decisions. When a Dwarf Fortress player tells the story of their doomed fortress, they are not recounting authored content -- they are narrating emergent events that arose from the interaction of hundreds of simulated systems. When a Rimworld player describes the dramatic rescue of a colonist, the emotional power comes from the stakes of their specific playthrough, not from a writer's script. Procedural narrative promises infinite replayability and genuine surprise, but it comes with significant limitations: emergent stories lack the thematic coherence, emotional pacing, and character depth of authored narratives. No procedural system has yet produced a story with the emotional impact of The Last of Us or the thematic sophistication of Disco Elysium. Today we study procedural narrative as both a design approach and a technical system: how games like Dwarf Fortress, Rimworld, and Middle-earth: Shadow of Mordor generate memorable stories from mechanics, and how authored and procedural approaches can be combined for the best of both worlds.
key_concepts: Emergent narrative: stories that arise from system interactions rather than authored scripts, unique to each playthrough
Simulation-driven storytelling: complex system interactions (Dwarf Fortress, Rimworld) that produce narratively meaningful events from mechanical rules
The Nemesis System (Shadow of Mordor): procedurally generated enemy identities, relationships, and grudges that create personalized narratives
Modular story components: pre-authored story fragments (events, consequences, character reactions) assembled procedurally based on context
Narrative lenses: systems that interpret mechanical events in narrative terms (a combat loss becomes a 'humiliating defeat' based on context variables)
Procedural character generation: creating NPC backgrounds, personalities, and relationships from randomized but constrained attribute systems
Authored + procedural hybrid: using procedural systems for world events and NPC behavior within an authored main storyline framework
The narrative coherence problem: procedural systems can generate surprising events but struggle with thematic consistency and satisfying arcs
Player as narrator: designing systems that produce events the player interprets and connects into their own narrative, leveraging human pattern-recognition
AI-assisted narrative: using language models for dynamic dialogue and narrative generation, with current capabilities and limitations
lecture_notes.demonstration: Building a simple procedural event system in Ink: creating a library of 20 modular event fragments with variable slots, defining character personality traits that filter available events, implementing a random event selector weighted by current narrative state, and running the system to generate three unique story sequences from the same components -- then analyzing which sequences feel narratively coherent and why
setup_requirements: Ink editor for procedural system implementation
Modular event template library (provided: 20 event fragments with variable slots)
Reference gameplay footage from Dwarf Fortress, Rimworld, and Shadow of Mordor

review.value: Stories arising from system interactions that no author scripted, unique to each playthrough and driven by the player's engagement with game mechanics
Shadow of Mordor's procedural enemy identity system that generates persistent rivalries, remembers player interactions, and creates personalized narrative through mechanical relationships
Pre-authored narrative fragments with variable slots that can be assembled procedurally based on context, combining authorial quality with procedural variety
The fundamental challenge of procedural narrative: generated events can be surprising and memorable but typically lack the thematic consistency and satisfying arc structure of authored stories

class_schedule.topic: Narrative Tools Pipeline: Ink, Twine, and Engine Integration
learning_objectives: Evaluate narrative middleware tools and select appropriate tooling for different project types
Implement dialogue and branching content in both Ink and Twine with state tracking
Integrate narrative scripts with game engine systems through API connections
Establish a writer-designer workflow that scales to team production
readings: Ink Documentation: Advanced Topics (tunnels, threads, functions)
Twine 2 Documentation: SugarCube and Harlowe format guides
Yarn Spinner documentation: Integration with Godot and Unity

lecture_notes.introduction: The narrative tools you choose shape what kinds of stories you can tell at scale. A solo developer using Twine makes different design decisions than a studio team using Articy:Draft with custom engine integration. Each tool embodies assumptions about workflow, team size, and project scope that affect every aspect of narrative production. Today we deep-dive into the technical capabilities of the major narrative tools, implement the same scene across multiple tools for direct comparison, and address the critical question of engine integration: how narrative scripts communicate with game code to spawn characters, trigger events, update UI, and modify the game world based on story progression. This is where narrative design meets software engineering, and understanding both sides of the integration boundary is essential for any narrative designer working in a team.
key_concepts: Ink advanced features: tunnels for reusable passages, threads for parallel narrative streams, external functions for engine communication
Twine format comparison: Harlowe (beginner-friendly, limited logic) vs SugarCube (full JavaScript access, complex state management)
Yarn Spinner architecture: node-based dialogue with built-in Unity and Godot integration, command system for triggering game events from dialogue
Articy:Draft: visual narrative design tool for large teams with database-driven content management, entity relationships, and export to custom formats
Engine integration patterns: callback functions triggered by narrative events, variable synchronization between narrative state and game state
Content pipeline: writer authors in narrative tool, exports to engine-readable format, designer integrates with game systems, QA tests all paths
Version control for narrative: managing Ink/Yarn files in Git, handling merge conflicts in branching narratives, branching strategy for parallel content development
Localization pipeline: structuring narrative content for translation, managing string tables, handling text expansion across languages
Writer-designer workflow: clear interface between narrative content (writer's domain) and system behavior (designer's domain) to prevent stepping on each other's work
Testing narrative content: automated path testing to verify all branches are reachable, all variables are set, and no dead ends exist
lecture_notes.demonstration: Implementing the same dialogue scene in three tools: a quest-giving NPC conversation with conditional branches, variable tracking, and an engine callback command -- first in Ink with external functions, then in Twine/SugarCube with JavaScript integration, then in Yarn Spinner with Godot commands; comparing the authoring experience, code complexity, and integration requirements of each; then running Ink's automatic testing to verify all paths are valid
setup_requirements: Ink with Inky editor
Twine 2 with SugarCube 2 format
Yarn Spinner with Godot integration sample project
Side-by-side comparison workspace

review.value: Specialized authoring tools (Ink, Yarn Spinner, Articy:Draft) that allow writers to create branching content integrated with game engines without writing engine code directly
The communication layer between narrative scripts and game code, using callbacks, variable synchronization, and command systems to make story events affect gameplay
The process of preparing narrative content for translation including string extraction, translator-friendly formatting, text expansion accommodation, and cultural adaptation
Tools that traverse all possible branches in a narrative script to verify reachability, variable correctness, and absence of dead-end nodes without manual playtesting

learning_objectives: Prepare narrative content for internationalization from the start of development
Handle text expansion, cultural adaptation, and variable insertion in localized dialogue
Design UI and narrative systems that accommodate different text lengths and reading directions
Evaluate localization quality and identify common localization failures in games
readings: The Game Localization Handbook by Heather Chandler, Ch. 3-5
Ink Documentation: Localization Support
Lost in Translation: Localization Failures in Games (Polygon article)

lecture_notes.introduction: Localization is not translation. Translation converts words from one language to another; localization adapts the entire experience for a different culture, including text, voice acting, UI layout, cultural references, humor, color symbolism, and legal requirements. For narrative-driven games, localization is particularly challenging because branching content multiplies the volume of translatable text, dynamic text insertion (player name, item names, gendered pronouns) creates grammatical challenges in inflected languages, and cultural references that resonate in one market may be meaningless or offensive in another. The cost of poor localization planning is devastating: retrofitting localization support into a narrative system that was designed without it requires rearchitecting the content pipeline, rewriting variable insertion logic, and potentially redesigning UI layouts. Today we study how to build localization-ready from the beginning: structuring content for extraction, handling the grammatical complexities of different languages, designing UI that accommodates text expansion, and managing the workflow of sending content to translators and integrating their work back into the game.
key_concepts: Internationalization (i18n) vs localization (l10n): i18n is engineering the system to support multiple languages; l10n is adapting content for specific locales
Text expansion: translated text is typically 30-40% longer than English source text (German, French) or 10-15% shorter (Chinese, Japanese), requiring flexible UI
String extraction: separating all player-visible text from code into string tables that translators can work on without touching source files
Variable insertion challenges: 'You picked up {count} {item}' breaks in languages with gendered nouns, plural rules, or different word order
ICU MessageFormat: a standardized syntax for handling plurals, gender, and select patterns across languages without code changes
Cultural adaptation: references, humor, color symbolism, gesture meaning, and naming conventions that require modification for different markets
Voice-over localization: lip-sync constraints, cultural performance differences, and the cost implications of full VO localization vs subtitles only
Right-to-left (RTL) support: Arabic and Hebrew require mirrored UI layouts, affecting dialogue boxes, menus, and text rendering
Localization testing: verifying translated content fits in UI, variables resolve correctly in all languages, and cultural adaptation is appropriate
Continuous localization: workflow for updating translations as the game content changes during development, not just at the end
lecture_notes.demonstration: Localizing a dialogue scene: taking an English Ink script with variable insertion and branching, extracting strings into a localization-ready format, demonstrating how the German translation expands text by 35% and breaks a UI dialogue box, fixing the UI to accommodate expansion, converting variable insertion to ICU MessageFormat to handle German gendered nouns, and showing how the same system handles Japanese (shorter text, different honorifics) without code changes
setup_requirements: Ink project with variable insertion (from previous sessions)
Sample translations in German and Japanese (provided by translator consultants)
ICU MessageFormat reference documentation
UI mockup showing text overflow issues

review.value: The increase in text length when translating from English to other languages (30-40% longer for German/French, shorter for CJK), requiring UI designed for flexible text containers
Separating all player-visible text from code into external string tables, enabling translators to work on content without accessing or understanding source code
A standardized syntax for handling plural rules, gender agreement, and select patterns in translatable strings, preventing hardcoded grammatical assumptions
Modifying content beyond direct translation to account for cultural differences in references, humor, symbolism, and social norms across target markets

learning_objectives: Design playtesting sessions specifically targeted at evaluating narrative effectiveness
Measure player emotional engagement, comprehension, and sense of agency through testing
Identify narrative problems that only appear through player testing, not author review
Iterate on narrative design based on observed player behavior and reported experience
readings: The Art of Game Design by Jesse Schell, Ch. 25-26
Narrative Playtesting: What to Look For (GDC 2018 talk notes)
Game Usability: Advancing the Player Experience, Ch. 8

lecture_notes.introduction: Narrative playtesting is distinct from gameplay playtesting because the metrics of success are different. In gameplay testing, you can measure completion rate, death count, and time-per-section. In narrative testing, the critical questions are experiential: did the player understand what was happening? Did they care about the characters? Did they feel their choices mattered? Did the story make emotional sense? These questions cannot be answered by analytics alone -- they require observation of player behavior, analysis of their decision-making process, and direct questioning about their experience. Today we study the specific methodology of narrative playtesting: what to observe, what questions to ask, how to identify narrative problems that are invisible to the author, and how to translate test findings into design changes. The most common narrative testing revelation is the gap between authorial intent and player reception: the emotional moment the writer crafted with care may land flat because the player arrived at it from an unexpected direction, missed critical context, or had a different mental model of the characters.
key_concepts: Narrative-specific testing goals: story comprehension, emotional engagement, agency perception, character connection, pacing satisfaction
Silent observation protocol: watching where players hesitate on choices, what they re-read, when they express emotion, and which options they consider before selecting
Think-aloud for narrative: asking players to narrate their decision-making process during choices, revealing their mental models of characters and consequences
Post-play interview questions: 'What did you think was going to happen?', 'Who did you care about most?', 'Which choice was hardest?', 'What would you do differently?'
Path analysis: tracking which branches players choose and why, identifying content that is never reached and choices that feel one-sided
Emotional beat mapping: charting the intended emotional arc against the player's reported experience to identify where the narrative fails to deliver
Comprehension checks: asking players to summarize the story so far at specific points to verify they understood key plot information
The 'but nobody did that' problem: discovering that a branch you spent weeks writing is never chosen because the alternative is obviously better
Iterative narrative revision: the cycle of test-observe-revise-retest, making minimal changes that address observed problems without introducing new ones
Multiple tester perspectives: different players bring different reading strategies, empathy levels, and genre expectations that reveal different narrative problems
lecture_notes.demonstration: Running a narrative playtest session in class: two volunteers play through the same branching Twine story while the class observes on a projected screen, recording choice hesitation points, emotional reactions, and moments of confusion; followed by separate post-play interviews revealing different interpretations and experiences; then a group analysis identifying the top 3 narrative problems and proposing specific revisions for each
setup_requirements: Student narrative projects in playable state
Observation forms (provided: choice tracking, emotion log, comprehension check)
Screen mirroring for class observation
Post-play interview question guide (provided)

review.value: Charting the writer's intended emotional arc against the player's actual reported experience at each story beat, revealing where narrative delivery fails to match authorial intent
Tracking which narrative branches players choose and why, identifying content that is never reached and choices that are perceived as one-sided or unclear
Asking players to summarize the story at specific points during testing to verify they understood key plot information needed for upcoming decisions

review.key: The Gap Between Intent and Reception
review.value: The common narrative testing discovery that the author's intended emotional or thematic impact does not match the player's actual experience, often due to missing context or unexpected arrival paths

learning_objectives: Present a completed interactive narrative experience with professional articulation of design decisions
Conduct a structured postmortem analyzing what succeeded and what failed in the project
Evaluate peer projects using the narrative design criteria developed throughout the course
Identify areas for continued growth in interactive narrative design
readings: Postmortem Best Practices (Gamasutra/Game Developer archives)
Game Narrative Portfolio: What Studios Look For (GDC Career Seminar notes)
The Game Narrative Toolbox Ch. 12 (Building Your Career)

lecture_notes.introduction: The final presentation is where your fifteen weeks of learning culminate in a single demonstration of mastery. Presenting interactive narrative work requires a different skill set from presenting visual art or code: you must articulate design decisions that are invisible to the player, explain structural choices that the audience cannot see without diagrams, and demonstrate the experience without spoiling it. Today each student presents their final project, walks through their narrative design document, demonstrates key branching moments live, and delivers a postmortem analysis. The postmortem is not just a retrospective exercise -- it is a professional practice used in every game studio to capture lessons learned and improve future projects. A good postmortem is honest about failures, specific about successes, and forward-looking about what to do differently. It demonstrates the self-awareness and analytical thinking that studios value in narrative designers. The peer evaluation component provides practice in giving constructive critique -- a skill as important as the design skills themselves, because narrative designers spend much of their careers evaluating and improving the work of others.
key_concepts: Presentation structure: project overview (30 seconds), design philosophy (1 minute), live demonstration of key moments (2-3 minutes), structural analysis (1 minute), postmortem (1-2 minutes), Q&A
Demonstrating interactive narrative: selecting 2-3 key branching moments that showcase the design's strengths without revealing all content
Structural diagrams for presentation: simplified visual representations of the branching architecture that communicate design decisions to non-writers
Postmortem format: 3 things that went well (with evidence), 3 things that went wrong (with root cause analysis), 3 things you would do differently
Peer evaluation criteria: narrative quality, structural design, technical implementation, creative achievement, presentation clarity
Portfolio preparation: how to present interactive narrative work to potential employers (playable builds, structural diagrams, writing samples, design documents)
Continued learning paths: game writing fellowships, narrative design communities, published resources for advanced study
Industry context: roles in narrative design (narrative designer, game writer, world builder, quest designer) and their distinct skill requirements
The writer's voice in games: finding your unique perspective and style within the collaborative context of game development
Feedback integration: processing peer critique constructively and identifying actionable improvements for portfolio revision
lecture_notes.demonstration: Model presentation by the instructor: presenting a sample interactive narrative project with structural diagram walkthrough, live play of two contrasting paths through the same story, honest postmortem analysis, and demonstration of how to handle audience questions about design decisions; followed by student presentations with timed format and peer evaluation forms
setup_requirements: Student final projects in playable/exportable state
Presentation display setup (projector/screen)
Peer evaluation forms (provided: criteria-based rubric with comment fields)
Timer for presentation pacing

review.value: A structured project reflection analyzing successes (with evidence), failures (with root cause analysis), and future improvements -- standard practice in professional game development
A simplified visual representation of branching architecture designed to communicate design decisions to an audience, not a technical document but a presentation aid
Structured critique using defined criteria (narrative quality, structural design, technical execution, creativity), practicing the feedback skills essential for collaborative game development
Presenting interactive narrative work with playable builds, structural diagrams, writing samples, and design documents -- the materials narrative design employers evaluate

questions.question: A designer is planning a narrative RPG with 50 hours of gameplay and a budget of 200,000 words of dialogue. Explain why a pure binary branching tree would be infeasible and describe an alternative structure that manages combinatorial growth while preserving meaningful player choice. Include word-budget reasoning.
questions.answer: A pure binary branching tree doubles content at every decision point. With 10 sequential binary choices, you need 1,024 unique paths. If each path averages 200 words per node, 10 levels deep with 1,024 endpoints requires far more than 200,000 words of unique content. A foldback structure solves this: branches diverge for a scene then reconverge at mandatory plot nodes. With 10 foldback segments of 3 variants each, you need approximately 30 unique segments (roughly 6,000 words per segment = 180,000 words) rather than 1,024 unique paths. State variables personalize dialogue within shared structural nodes, adding perceived variety without unique branches.

rubric.key: Correctly identifies exponential growth problem with numbers
Names and accurately describes a feasible alternative structure
Provides word-budget calculation supporting feasibility argument

questions.question: Explain the concept of 'story gates' in interactive narrative design. Why are they necessary for maintaining structural integrity, and how do they interact with player agency? Give an example of a story gate from a game you have played.
questions.answer: Story gates are mandatory narrative beats that all player paths must pass through regardless of branching choices. They are necessary because without them, different paths could diverge so far that the narrative loses coherence -- a player on one path might miss critical information that later scenes assume they have. Story gates maintain structural integrity by ensuring all players share certain key experiences while allowing freedom between gates. They interact with agency by constraining it at specific moments while maximizing it elsewhere. Example: in The Witcher 3, the Battle of Kaer Morhen is a story gate -- regardless of which allies the player recruited (branching), the battle itself must occur (gate), though its outcome varies based on prior choices (agency within the gate).

rubric.key: Correctly defines story gates as mandatory narrative beats
Explains why they are necessary for coherence with agency trade-off
Provides a specific valid game example with analysis

questions.question: Compare state-variable-based narrative personalization with pure branching. Describe a specific scenario where state variables achieve equivalent perceived player agency with significantly less authored content.
questions.answer: Pure branching creates separate content paths for each choice combination; state variables modify shared content based on accumulated choice data. Scenario: an NPC greeting the player in Chapter 5 that references Chapter 2 choices. With pure branching, you need 8 unique greeting scenes (one for each combination of 3 binary choices). With state variables, you write one greeting scene with conditional lines: 'if helped_village: You're the one who saved us / else: I heard what happened to the village.' Each conditional adds 1-2 lines rather than an entire scene. For 3 binary variables with one conditional line each, you write 1 scene with 6 conditional lines instead of 8 complete scenes -- roughly 90% content reduction with equivalent perceived responsiveness.

rubric.key: Correctly distinguishes pure branching from state variable approach
Provides specific scenario with content volume comparison
Demonstrates equivalent perceived agency with quantified content savings

quizzes.topic: Dialogue Systems, World-Building, and Environmental Storytelling

questions.question: Compare bark systems and full dialogue trees as NPC communication mechanisms. For each, describe a scenario where it is the superior choice and explain why the alternative would be inappropriate.
questions.answer: Bark systems deliver short context-sensitive lines without pulling the player out of gameplay; superior for ambient world-building during exploration or combat because they maintain flow (e.g., guards commenting on weather or player faction). A full dialogue tree here would force the player to stop walking, enter a conversation UI, and click through menus just to hear 'Nice weather today' -- inappropriate because it breaks pacing for trivial content. Full dialogue trees are superior for pivotal narrative decisions like negotiating an alliance, because the player needs multi-turn exchange, information revelation, and branching consequences. Using barks here would strip agency from a critical story moment -- a guard shouting 'You convinced me!' without the player having any input is not storytelling.

rubric.key: Correctly characterizes both systems with key distinctions
Bark system scenario with clear reasoning about pacing
Dialogue tree scenario with reasoning about agency and consequence

questions.question: Design the environmental storytelling for a single room in a post-apocalyptic game. Without using any text, dialogue, or UI, describe at least four specific objects or arrangements that communicate a family survived here for months before being forced to leave suddenly. Explain what each element communicates.
questions.answer: Four elements: (1) Tally marks scratched into a wall spanning several months, communicating duration of occupation through systematic timekeeping. (2) A child's drawings taped to a boarded-up window depicting a family and sunshine, communicating that a family with children lived here and attempted normalcy. (3) Organized supply shelves half-emptied with some items knocked to the floor, communicating hasty departure mid-packing -- the organization shows months of careful living, the scattered items show the ending was rushed. (4) A barricaded door with the barricade pushed aside from inside (furniture displaced inward), communicating the family broke through their own defenses to flee, meaning the threat came from within or behind them, not through the door they were defending.

rubric.key: Four distinct environmental elements described with specificity
Each element clearly communicates specific narrative information
Elements collectively tell coherent story of prolonged stay and sudden departure without text

questions.question: Explain the 'iceberg principle' of world-building. Why must a narrative designer know more about the world than the player will ever see? Describe a practical consequence of violating this principle.
questions.answer: The iceberg principle states that the creator must develop world-building depth far beyond what is directly presented to the audience. Only 10-20% of world-building should be directly visible; the rest is invisible foundation. This invisible depth matters because it ensures consistency: every visible detail, NPC behavior, and plot point is informed by the same underlying world logic. When a player asks an NPC about history, the writer draws from the established timeline rather than improvising. A practical consequence of violating it: two quests written by different team members contradict each other about the same faction's motives because no shared world bible exists. The player encounters both quests and perceives the world as incoherent and carelessly constructed, breaking immersion and trust in the narrative.

rubric.key: Correctly explains the iceberg principle with the depth-beyond-visibility concept
Explains why invisible depth ensures consistency
Describes a specific practical consequence of violation

quizzes.topic: Character Writing, Player Agency, and Choice Design

questions.question: Distinguish between a defined protagonist and a blank-slate avatar. For each, describe one advantage and one limitation for interactive storytelling. Name a game example of each and explain how it handles the limitation you identified.
questions.answer: A defined protagonist has pre-established personality and voice (advantage: enables deep authored character moments and consistent voice; limitation: player may disagree with character's reactions to their choices). Example: Geralt in The Witcher 3 -- handles the limitation by offering dialogue choices that represent different facets of Geralt's established personality rather than letting the player act completely out of character. A blank-slate avatar has minimal authored personality (advantage: maximum player projection and identification; limitation: difficulty creating emotional character moments because the character has no defined personality to emote from). Example: the Courier in Fallout: New Vegas -- handles the limitation by using companion characters who react emotionally to events on behalf of the player, providing the emotional content the avatar itself cannot express.

rubric.key: Correct definitions with clear advantage and limitation for each
Valid game examples that match the type described
Explains how each game handles the identified limitation

questions.question: Define 'meaningful choice' in interactive narrative using at least four criteria. Then analyze a specific choice from a game you have played, evaluating it against your criteria and identifying whether it succeeds or fails at creating genuine player agency.
questions.answer: Meaningful choice criteria: (1) Clear options -- the player understands what each choice represents. (2) Distinct outcomes -- different choices lead to observably different consequences. (3) Value alignment -- the choice engages the player's personal values or goals. (4) Irreversibility -- the choice cannot be undone, giving it weight. (5) Visibility -- consequences are shown to the player, confirming the choice mattered. Analysis: Mass Effect 2's loyalty missions feature a final choice (e.g., Jack's loyalty mission: destroy or preserve the Cerberus facility). Clear options (yes), distinct outcomes (Jack's loyalty vs Cerberus intel), value alignment (justice vs pragmatism), irreversible (yes), visible consequences (Jack's dialogue changes, survival in final mission affected). This succeeds at meaningful choice by meeting all criteria -- the player feels genuine weight because the consequence is both immediate (Jack's reaction) and delayed (final mission outcome).
